headline: Chronic pain moves into the brain's emotion centres
subtext: Brain scans show where long-term pain really lives.
steps: Early back pain lit up the brain's sensory pain regions.
After a year, persistent pain had shifted into emotion-related circuits.
The pain becomes less about the body and more about the brain's learned response.
caption:
When pain becomes chronic, it changes address in the brain.

In a landmark study, researchers scanned people with new back pain and followed them for a year. Early on, pain activated the brain's sensory pain regions, as you would expect. But in those whose pain persisted, the activity migrated into emotion-related circuits. The same complaint, back pain, was now being generated by a different part of the brain.

This is neuroplasticity in action. It helps explain why long-term pain in conditions like fibromyalgia, IBS, tinnitus and long COVID is so tied to stress, mood and memory, and why recovery works through the nervous system rather than the site of the symptom.

It also carries hope: circuits that learned pain can be helped to unlearn it. Begin at https://neurowaze.health/start-my-recovery

#ChronicPain #Neuroplasticity #Fibromyalgia #IBS #Tinnitus #LongCovid #MindBody #PainRecovery #Neurowaze
first_comment: The study: Hashmi JA et al. (2013), Shape shifting pain: chronification of back pain shifts brain representation from nociceptive to emotional circuits, Brain, 136(9), 2751 to 2768. fMRI showed pain representation moving from sensory to emotional brain circuits as back pain became chronic. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23983029/
